coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence and photothermal dual-mode immunosensor based on MnO2 nanosheets for the detection of isocarbophos
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jie
Deng, Hao
Jia, Bao-Zhu
Lin, Ze-Shan
Wang, Yu
Wang, Hong
Xu, Zhen-Lin
Luo, Lin
coredata.dc:description: Isocarbophos (ICP) is a typical highly toxic organophosphorus pesticide, and its rapid and accurate detection is essential for food safety. In this study, we presented a ratiometric fluorescence and photothermal dual-mode immunoassay for the quantification of ICP in food samples, leveraging the oxidase-like activity and photothermal effect of manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2NSs). The immunoassay utilized alkaline phosphatase (ALP) as a labeling enzyme, which efficiently catalyzes the dephosphorylation of ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AA2P) to produce ascorbic acid (AA). The generated AA subsequently initiated the decomposition of MnO2NSs, resulting in a reduction of both the photothermal signal from MnO2NSs and the fluorescence signal from oxidized Amplex Red (oxAR), while moderately restoring the fluorescence emission from blue-emissive carbon dots (b-CDs). The limits of detection (LOD) were as low as 2.62 pg mL−1 in photothermal mode and 0.21 pg mL−1 in ratiometric fluorescence mode. Recovery rates from spiked food samples were 94.8–119.8 % in photothermal mode and 97.9–105.5 % in ratiometric fluorescence mode. This study creates promising new avenues for pesticide detection and establishes an effective platform for dual-mode immunosensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-mode immunosensor
Ratiometric fluorescence
Photothermal detection
Manganese dioxide nanosheets

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection mechanism of the dual-mode immunosensor
Performance of the dual-mode immunosensor for ICP detection
Selectivity and anti-interference evaluation of the dual-mode immunosensor
Detection of ICP in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2023A1515030011), the Foundation for Distinguished Young Talents in Higher Education of Guangdong (2022KQNCX055), the Research Fund Program of Key Laboratory of Tropical Fruit and Vegetable Cold-chain of Hainan Province/Institute of Agro-products Processing and Design (HAAS2023PT0210).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of MnO2NSs. (A) TEM image. (B) Full-range XPS spectra of MnO2NSs. (C) XPS spectra of Mn 2P1/2 and Mn 2P3/2. (D) XRD patterns of MnO2NSs. (E) Temperature change of MnO2NSs with concentrations of 0, 10, 50, 100, 250 μg mL−1 under 808 nm NIR irradiation. (F) Temperature change of MnO2NSs (100 μg mL−1) for three lasers on/off cycles. (G) The UV–vis absorbance spectra of (a) TMB, (b) MnO2NSs, (c) TMB + Mn2+, (d) TMB + MnO2NSs, and (e) TMB + MnO2NSs + AA. (H) The UV–vis absorption spectra of MnO2NSs (Brown line) and the fluorescence excitation (Red line) and emission spectra (Blue line) of b-CDs. (I) The FL lifetime of b-CDs with or without MnO2NSs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Feasibility of the dual-mode immunosensor. (A) The feasibility of ratiometric fluorescence signal. Comparison of the stability of ratiometric fluorescence signal (F455/F585) and single emission signals (F455 or F585) in the assay of six parallel samples (B), and extended detection time (C). (D) Fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric fluorescence system in the presence of different concentrations of AA. (E) The relationship between the ratio of the fluorescence intensity (F455/F585) versus the concentration of AA. (F) The linear relationship between temperature change and the logarithm of AA concentrations. (G) Fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric fluorescence system in the presence of different concentrations of ALP. (H) The relationship between the F455/F585 versus the concentration of ALP. (I) The linear relationship between temperature change and the logarithm of ALP concentrations. The results obtained were expressed as the average ± standard deviation from at least three repeats.

Optimization of conditions and evaluation of the sensitivity of the dual-mode immunosensor. Effect of AA2P concentration (A), MnO2NSs concentration (B), Tris-HCl pH (C), and incubation time (D) on the sensitivity of ratiometric fluorescence system. The influence of different irradiation times on the photothermal system (E) and the optimization of the concentration of coating antigen and antibody (F). (G) Fluorescence emission spectra of the ratiometric fluorescence system at different ICP concentrations. (H) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio and the logarithm of ICP concentrations (n = 3). (I) Linear relationship between temperature change and logarithm of ICP concentrations (n = 3), the insets are photothermal images of the solutions in the presence of different concentrations of ICP. The results obtained were expressed as the average ± standard deviation from at least three repeats.

Anti-interference and selectivity evaluation of the dual-mode immunosensor and matrix effects of samples. The responses of F455/F585 (A) and the temperature variation (B) against ICP or other interfering substances. The responses of F455/F585 (C) and the temperature variation (D) against ICP or other control pesticides. The concentration of ICP in the fluorescent system was 5 ng mL−1, and that in the photothermal system was 1 μg mL−1. Concentrations of other effectors were 10 times the corresponding ICP concentrations, respectively. (E–G) Matrix solutions with different dilution factors. The results obtained were expressed as the average ± standard deviation from at least three repeats.
Schematic illustration of the dual-mode immunosensor.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence and photothermal dual-mode immunosensor based on MnO2 nanosheets for the detection of isocarbophos

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors are equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Isocarbophos (ICP) is a typical highly toxic organophosphorus pesticide, and its rapid and accurate detection is essential for food safety. In this study, we presented a ratiometric fluorescence and photothermal dual-mode immunoassay for the quantification of ICP in food samples, leveraging the oxidase-like activity and photothermal effect of manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2NSs). The immunoassay utilized alkaline phosphatase (ALP) as a labeling enzyme, which efficiently catalyzes the dephosphorylation of ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AA2P) to produce ascorbic acid (AA). The generated AA subsequently initiated the decomposition of MnO2NSs, resulting in a reduction of both the photothermal signal from MnO2NSs and the fluorescence signal from oxidized Amplex Red (oxAR), while moderately restoring the fluorescence emission from blue-emissive carbon dots (b-CDs). The limits of detection (LOD) were as low as 2.62 pg mL−1 in photothermal mode and 0.21 pg mL−1 in ratiometric fluorescence mode. Recovery rates from spiked food samples were 94.8–119.8 % in photothermal mode and 97.9–105.5 % in ratiometric fluorescence mode. This study creates promising new avenues for pesticide detection and establishes an effective platform for dual-mode immunosensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datasets utilized in this study are available in online repositories. The specific names of the repository or repositories, as well as the corresponding accession number(s), can be found in the article or Supplementary Materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Isocarbophos (ICP), a widely used organophosphorus pesticide, has values in agriculture for its broad-spectrum insecticidal efficacy and its ability to significantly enhance crop yield [1]. However, improper application can lead to residues on agricultural products, posing serious ecological risks and increasing the potential for chronic and acute toxicity in humans [2,3]. Due to these health hazards, China has prohibited the production of ICP and its related formulations, with a complete ban on its sale and use taking effect on September 1, 2024 [4]. Therefore, reliable detection of ICP is critical for ensuring food safety and protecting human health.

Instrumental techniques such as GC–MS/MS (Gas Chromatography-Tandem Mass Spectrometry) and LC-MS/MS (Liquid Chromatograph-Tandem Mass Spectrometry) are well established standard methods for pesticide residue detection with high accuracy and reliability [5,6]. However, these methods are often unsuitable for on-site detection due to complex sample preparation requirements and the need for trained personnel. Alternatively, immunoassays have gained traction as practical solutions for pesticide monitoring thanks to their cost-effectiveness and high specificity [7]. Nevertheless, traditional ELISA (Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay) can sometimes yield false-negative results due to its moderate sensitivity and dependence on single signal outputs [8,9]. To mitigate these limitations, several strategies have been introduced. One effective approach involves enhancing assay sensitivity by replacing conventional colorimetric signals with more sensitive alternatives [10,11]. Moreover, developing systems that utilize dual or multiple signal outputs has shown significant promise [12,13]. These multi-mode sensors offer unique responses to external interferences, providing a foundation for portable detection [14,15]. By enabling self-correction, such multi-mode sensors mitigate interference, thereby improving the accuracy and reliability of the results, and can be tailored to meet varied sensitivity and instrumentation needs [16]. Accordingly, developing a multi-mode immunoassay for the sensitive and accurate detection of ICP can help ensure food safety and protect human health.

To date, several multi-mode sensors have been constructed utilizing nanomaterial composites [17,18]. However, further refinement in composite methodologies is essential, particularly with respect to structural integrity and functional performance [19]. Utilizing the multifaceted sensing properties of a single material to generate multiple signal outputs remains an attractive approach. Among advanced materials,

two-dimensional metal oxide nanomaterials have emerged as key candidates for biosensing platforms due to their unique physical and chemical properties, spurring significant interest in this domain [20,21]. In particular, manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2NSs) have garnered particular attention due to the advantages they afford [22], including high loading capacity [23], broad UV absorption band [24], excellent oxidase-like activity [25], redox potential [26], and photothermal effect [27]. For instance, Jiang et al. utilized the redox and photothermal properties of MnO2NSs to enable the ratiometric fluorescent and photothermal detection of organophosphorus pesticides via the enzyme inhibition method [28]. Similarly, Lv et al. developed a fluorescent immunosensor for detecting fenitrothion by employing MnO2NSs as both a fluorescence quencher and a carrier for gold nanoclusters [29]. Nonetheless, reports on multi-mode immunosensors that leverage the diverse properties of MnO2NSs remain limited.

Building on the above insights, this study employed the redox properties, oxidase-like activity, fluorescence quenching capacity, and photothermal effect of MnO2NSs to develop a dual-mode immunosensor for the detection of ICP. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the ALP enzyme-labeled secondary antibody (ALP-Ab2), a standard enzyme in ELISA capable of catalyzing the conversion of ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AA2P) to ascorbic acid (AA), served as a mediator between the immune response and the signal generation. Upon exposure to positive samples, the amount of coated antigen–antibody complexes immobilized on the microplate was reduced, resulting in less ALP for AA2P dephosphorylation, and subsequently less AA production. This decrease in AA limited MnO2NSs decomposition to Mn2+. Consequently, more MnO2NSs facilitated the oxidation of Amplex Red (AR) to generate red fluorescence and quenched the blue emission from blue-emissive carbon dots (b-CDs), establishing a ratiometric fluorescence signal. Additionally, because there were more MnO2NSs, a higher temperature signal was produced under 808 nm NIR laser irradiation.

ICP and other organophosphorus pesticide standards were obtained from Tanmo standard center (Beijing, China). ALP-Ab2 was obtained from Earthox (California, America). Alkaline phosphatase, ovalbumin (OVA) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were provided by Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). AR was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). AA, AA2P, MnCl2 and tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were supplied by Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) was procured from Jianyang biotechnology Co., Ltd (Guangzhou, China). KMnO4 was purchased from Kaixin chemical industry Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). NaCl, CaCl2, MgSO4, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, Na2HPO4, acetonitrile and hydrogen peroxide were procured from Damao Tianjin Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Hydrochloric acid was obtained from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Guangzhou, China). The antigen (ICP-BSA) and monoclonal antibody (ICP-mAb) against ICP and the b-CDs were previously prepared in our lab [30,31].

ELISA plates were washed by a Multiskan MK2 microplate washer (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Spectra of UV absorption and fluorescence were recorded by a SpectraMax i3x multi-function microplate reader (Molecular Devices, USA). Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry was performed using a Thermo Scientific TSQ 8000 Evo (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America).

MnO2NSs were synthesized following literature [32] with slight modifications. Concisely, a solution containing 1.8 M of H2SO4 (45 μL) and 461.4 mg of SDS (32 mM) was mixed with 50 mL of distilled water and then refluxed for 15 min at 95 °C. Next, 50 mM of KMnO4 solution (1.6 mL) was rapidly added to the above mixture solution for 60 min. The crude product was then centrifuged and washed three times with ethanol and ultrapure water. Finally, the MnO2NSs were resuspended in ultrapure water and kept at 4 °C.

For AA detection: 50 μL of MnO2NSs, 50 μL of b-CDs, and 50 μL of AR were added to the series concentrations of AA solutions (50 μL, Tris-HCl buffer). Following a reaction at 37 °C for 30 min, the fluorescence intensity of b-CDs (F455) and AR (F585) was recorded separately, and the AA concentration was quantified by the fluorescence intensity ratio (F455/F585).

For ALP detection: AA2P (50 μL) was added to the series of concentrations of ALP solutions (50 μL, Tris-HCl buffer) and reacted at 37 °C for 40 min to form AA. Followed by the same steps as AA detection, ALP activity was determined by F455/F585.

The procedure for the detection of ICP by the dual-mode immunosensor was as follows: the coated antigen (ICP-BSA, 100 μL/well) diluted in carbonate buffer was added to the black microplate and coated for 12 h at 4 °C, washed with PBST buffer for two times, and 120 μL of blocking buffer was added to each well and blocked for 3 h at 37 °C. Next, it was stored at 4 °C for subsequent use after discarding the blocking buffer. Different concentrations of ICP (50 μL, PBS buffer) and 50 μL of ICP-mAb were successively added to the coated microplate. Five washing steps were performed after incubation at 37 °C for 40 min, and ALP-Ab2 (100 μL) was added to each well for 30 min and then washed five times. Then AA2P (100 μL) was added at 37 °C for 40 min.

For ratiometric fluorescence analysis: 50 μL each of MnO2NSs, b-CDs, and AR solution were added to the above mixed solution and placed at 37 °C for 30 min. After that, the fluorescence of b-CDs (F455) and AR (F585) was recorded, respectively, and a calibration curve was constructed by F455/F585.

For photothermal analysis: First, transfer the liquid in the black microplate to the transparent one. Add 100 μL of MnO2NSs solution and placed at 37 °C for 30 min. After being irradiated with a near-infrared light (808 nm) (FU1064AL50-GD16, Shenzhen Fu-Ram Technology Co., Ltd) for 3 min. The photothermal signals of the solution were recorded by a cell phone thermal imaging camera (HM-TJP10-3AMF, Hangzhou Microfilm Software Co., Ltd). The specific detection model was detailed in the supporting information (Fig. S1).

Apple and cowpea samples were purchased from local shopping malls, and tap water samples were obtained from our laboratory. According to the national standard of China (GB 23200.113-2018), the concrete pretreatment was performed as follows: Weigh 10.0 g of sample homogenate in a 50 mL centrifuge tube, add acetonitrile (10 mL), magnesium sulfate (4.0 g), sodium chloride (1.0 g), sodium citrate (1.0 g), and disodium hydrogen citrate (0.5 g), then shake for 1 min and centrifuge. Afterwards, pipette 6 mL of supernatant into a 15 mL centrifuge tube containing 900 mg of magnesium sulfate and 150 mg of PSA, then mix well and centrifuge. 2 mL of supernatant was removed and de-nitrogen blew to near dryness, and it was resuspended in 1 mL of PBS to obtain the sample extract. In addition, in order to verify the accuracy and reliability of the dual-mode immunosensor, the concentration of ICP in the spiked sample was also determined by GC–MS. The specific parameters and conditions were detailed in the supporting information.

The morphology and particle size of the synthesized MnO2NSs were observed by TEM, and the element composition, valence state, and crystal structure were characterized by XPS and XRD. A very thin, two-dimensional lamellar structure with folds was seen from the TEM image (Fig. 1 A). The full-range XPS spectra of MnO2NSs (Fig. 1B) revealed the existence of the elements O and Mn, which were represented by two prominent peaks at 642.0 and 529.6 eV, respectively. In the Mn 2P spectra (Fig. 1C), there are two distinct peaks belonging to Mn 2P3/2 and Mn 2P1/2, located at 642.0 and 653.0 eV, respectively [33]. Additionally, the XRD patterns were used to analyze the crystal structure of MnO2NSs. As shown in Fig. 1D, there were four peaks at 2θ = 11.96, 24.18, 36.57, and 65.79°, which matched the characteristic peaks of δ-MnO2 (PDF No. 18-0802). The aforementioned evidence indicated the successful synthesis of MnO2NSs.

Moreover, the photothermal effect of MnO2NSs itself is the critical property for generating photothermal signals in this experiment. Fig. 1E demonstrated that when exposed to an 808 nm NIR laser, the temperature of the solution rose considerably with the increasing concentration of the MnO2NSs, and it became stable within 5 min. Thus, the photothermal conversion ability of MnO2NSs can be convincingly evidenced after excluding the negligible temperature difference caused by the buffer. Besides, by irradiating the same solution for three cycles, an almost constant temperature difference reflected the photothermal stability of MnO2NSs (Fig. 1F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of the dual-mode immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a dual-mode immunosensor was developed based on the specific interaction between antigen and antibody. This sensor leveraged four key properties of MnO2NSs: redox ability, fluorescence quenching ability, oxidase-like activity, and photothermal effect. Remarkably, these properties are not available in Mn2+. As a mediator between immune recognition and signal output, ALP-Ab2 could dephosphorylate AA2P to generate AA. The generated AA then regulated the concentration of MnO2NSs in the system, leading to a change in fluorescence and photothermal signals. The reduced Mn2+ was unable to oxidize AR and restored the fluorescence intensity of b-CDs instead. At the same time, under 808 nm NIR laser irradiation, the reaction solution reflected a lower temperature, which was attributed to the poor photothermal effect of Mn2+.

To illustrate the construction principle of this dual-mode immunosensor, the subsequent experiments commenced with the performance verification of MnO2NSs. First of all, the oxidase-like activity of MnO2NSs is the key point of many current studies [34,35] and is also evidenced by the color change of TMB in Fig. 1G. Oxidized TMB produced a clearly identifiable UV absorption peak at 652 nm after the addition of MnO2NSs, which proved that the oxidation reaction can be accomplished without the need for hydrogen peroxide. After adding a certain concentration of AA, the blue originally obtained from the oxidation was returned to colorlessness. Combined with the changes in UV absorbance of MnO2NSs under a gradient concentration of AA (Fig. S3), it is affirmed that AA can alter the oxidation activity of MnO2NSs by means of reductive decomposition.

Additionally, the fluorescence quenching ability of MnO2NSs was investigated. As depicted in Fig. 1H, the synthesized MnO2NSs exhibited UV absorption within the range of 300–600 nm, which overlapped with the fluorescence excitation peak (Ex: 350 nm) and emission peak (Em: 455 nm) of b-CDs in a large area. With the increasing concentration of MnO2NSs, the fluorescence response of b-CDs decreased steadily (Fig. S5), and even the inhibition rate reached 97 % at 1 mg mL−1 of MnO2NSs. This indicated the possibility of the inner filter effect (IFE) between MnO2NSs and b-CDs [26]. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetimes of b-CDs with or without MnO2NSs were evaluated in both cases (Fig. 1I). It was noted from the results that the average fluorescence lifetime of b-CDs was 7.47 ns, which did not change markedly when MnO2NSs were added (t = 7.87 ns). This result further confirmed the assumption that the quenching of fluorescence between MnO2NSs and b-CDs was based on the IFE.

After successfully validating the sensing characteristics of MnO2NSs, the feasibility of whether the dual-mode sensor could trigger a signal change was determined. In terms of ratiometric fluorescence signal, as shown in Fig. 2 A, when MnO2NSs, b-CDs, and AR coexisted, the fluorescence of b-CDs would be quenched. Simultaneously, MnO2NSs oxidized non-fluorescent AR to fluorescent oxidized Amplex Red (oxAR), producing a robust fluorescence at 585 nm. In addition, there were no interactions between b-CDs and AR (Fig. S6). Following the introduction of AA, the fluorescence intensity of b-CDs showed a minor recovery as the MnO2NSs were decomposed into Mn2+. In contrast, oxAR exhibited a sharp decline in fluorescence, dropping to nearly no fluorescence. This indicated that the sensitivity of the two substances to the concentration of MnO2NSs was distinctive.

To further confirm these observations, an experiment was performed using gradient concentrations of AA within two single-emission fluorescence systems: MnO2NSs/AR and MnO2NSs/b-CDs. As shown in Fig. S7, AA concentrations above 312.5 μM primarily affected the fluorescence intensity of b-CDs, while AR did not fluoresce due to the decomposition of MnO2NSs. Conversely, at lower AA concentrations, b-CDs provided a nearly constant reference signal, with the fluorescence change of oxAR becoming the dominant factor (Fig. 2D). Thus, when detecting trace amounts of AA, the ternary MnO2NSs/b-CDs/AR system did not produce a significant opposing fluorescence response. However, considering signal stability, the experiment compared single-emission signals with the ratiometric signal using parallel samples and varying reaction times. As shown in Fig. 2B, the relative standard deviations (RSDs) for the ratiometric signal (F455/F585) and the single signals (F455 and F585) were 6.5 %, 10.4 %, and 10.9 %, respectively, for six parallel samples. Additionally, samples from the same batch were tested post-reaction to evaluate long-term signal stability. As shown in Fig. 2C, the RSDs for the ratiometric signal were 6.4 %, compared to 6.8 % and 13.1 % for F455 and F585, respectively. These results demonstrated the high stability of the ratiometric fluorescence system with MnO2NSs/b-CDs/AR. In the AA concentration range of 60–300 μM, two linear relationships were observed between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F455/F585) and AA concentration, with a cut-off point at 100 μM (Fig. 2E). The regression equations were y = 0.154 × − 12.865 (AA: 100–300 μM, R2 = 0.981) and y = 0.044 × − 1.600 (AA: 60–100 μM, R2 = 0.990). The limit of detection (LOD) according to the 3d/s rule (d is the standard deviation of the blank and s is the slope of the standard curve; all the LODs in this paper were estimated based on this rule) was 35.72 nM. In photothermal mode, the temperature difference was inversely correlated with the logarithmic concentrations of AA in the range of 31.25–250 μM (Fig. 2F), and the linear regression equation was y = -21.701 [logAA] + 59.267 (R2 = 0.991), and the LOD was 0.80 μM.

Upon discovering that AA could deconstruct MnO2NSs and alter the signaling, a strategy was devised to use AA2P as a substrate for ALP, converting it into AA [36]. In line with the prediction, both the ratiometric fluorescence signal and the photothermal signal showed good linear correlations for a certain ALP concentration. Specifically, F455/F585 showed a positive linear correlation with ALP concentration in the range of 25–450 mU mL−1 (Fig. 2G–H). The regression equation was y = 0.0233 × − 0.406 (R2 = 0.992), and the LOD was 0.57 mU mL−1. Additionally, the inset of Fig. 2I demonstrated that as the ALP concentration increased, the concentration of MnO2NSs in the sensor decreased, leading to a lower solution temperature. Consequently, a negative correlation was observed between the temperature difference and the logarithmic ALP concentration within the range of 0.98 to 1000 mU mL−1 (Fig. 2I), with a linear regression equation of y = -2.513 [logALP] + 24.907 (R2 = 0.984), and a LOD was 0.22 mU mL−1. These results confirmed the feasibility of the detection mechanism for the dual-mode detection system based on MnO2NSs, paving the way for the development of a dual-mode immunosensor for ICP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the dual-mode immunosensor for ICP detection
ce:section.ce:para: For optimal detection performance, various key parameters were fine-tuned, including the concentrations of AA2P and MnO2NSs, the pH of the Tris-HCl buffer, and the reaction time. (F455/F585) and (F455/F585)0 represented the fluorescence intensity ratio in the presence and absence of ALP-Ab2 in the system, respectively. A higher value of (F455/F585)/(F455/F585)0 indicated a more significant signal change, leading to a more sensitive sensor.

With the addition of AA2P, more AA was produced through dephosphorylation while keeping other variables constant. This led to the recovery of b-CDs fluorescence and a reduction in oxAR fluorescence due to the further reduction of MnO2NSs. Notably, compared to the same concentration of AA, AA2P had no direct impact on the signal output (Fig. S8). This resulted in a positive correlation between AA2P concentration and (F455/F585)/(F455/F585)0. As shown in Fig. 3 A, when the AA2P concentration exceeded 250 μM, the fluorescence system reached dynamic equilibrium, and the growth rate (F455/F585)/(F455/F585)0 was almost unchanged. Thus, 250 μM was set as the optimal concentration. The concentration of MnO2NSs was crucial in determining the intensity of the fluorescence signal. Within a specific range, increasing the concentration of MnO2NSs would produce a slight quenching effect on b-CDs, whereas the generation of oxAR would significantly intensify F585, leading to a continual decrease in the value of F455/F585. However, in the absence of ALP-Ab2, the reduction step was eliminated, allowing MnO2NSs to fully participate in the subsequent quenching of b-CDs fluorescence and AR oxidation. As a result, the change in (F455/F585)0 was faster compared to F455/F585, leading to quicker saturation. This was why the highest growth rate was observed at a concentration of 50 μg mL−1 of MnO2NSs (Fig. 3B). In enzymatic reactions, pH directly influences the catalytic activity of enzymes. ALP exhibited greater enzymatic activity in an alkaline environment, but AR became unstable at pH levels above 8.5 [37]. After excluding the effect of generated AA on the pH of the reaction solution (Fig. S9), the maximum growth rate was observed when the Tris-HCl buffer pH was 7.5 (Fig. 3C). Additionally, the reaction time of the fluorescence system and the duration of laser irradiation were evaluated (Fig. 3D and E). Considering the growth rate changes, correlation coefficients among the variables, and cost-effectiveness, the optimal reaction conditions were determined as follows: 250 μM AA2P, 50 μg mL−1 MnO2NSs, Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.5), 30-min fluorescence reaction, and 808 nm NIR laser irradiation for 3 min. To achieve the best detection performance for ICP, the working concentrations of ICP-BSA and ICP-mAb were optimized. As shown in Fig. 3F, the highest detection sensitivity, with a half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of 2.88 ng mL−1, was achieved when the concentrations of ICP-BSA and ICP-mAb were 250 ng mL−1 and 4 μg mL−1, respectively.

Under the optimized conditions, the dual-mode detection of ICP was performed by analyzing the changes in fluorescence signals at 455 nm and 585 nm, along with the temperature change of the solution. As shown in Fig. 3G, the increase in the concentration of ICP led to a corresponding enhancement of the red fluorescence at 585 nm, while the blue fluorescence at 455 nm exhibited a minor decrease. Also, there was a linear correlation between F455/F585 and the logarithmic concentration of ICP in the range of 0.24 to 7.81 ng mL−1 (Fig. 3H). The equation for this relationship was y = -0.484 [logICP] + 2.10 (R2 = 0.982), and the LOD according to the 3d/s rule was 0.21 pg mL−1. A satisfactory linear correlation between temperature difference and the logarithmic concentration of ICP was obtained by photothermal analysis in the range of 41.15 to 10000 ng mL−1 with an equation of y = 4.394 [logICP] − 5.736 (R2 = 0.991), with a LOD of 2.62 pg mL−1 (Fig. 3I). Among these, the ratiometric fluorescence signal offered a lower LOD whereas the photothermal signal provided a broader linear range. And the detection performance of this dual-mode immunosensor was superior to or comparable with most reported aptamer sensors and immunoassays (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference evaluation of the dual-mode immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure the practical application of the established dual-mode immunosensor, it was also evaluated in terms of both anti-interference and specificity in this study. To anti-interference, the change in fluorescence ratio of F455/F585 and the temperature change in solution were compared by the introduction of a series of potential bio-interfering substances (Na+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Cl−, PO4 2−, Glu, BSA, and OVA). Fig. 4 A and B demonstrated that adding other interfering compounds did not have an obvious effect on both signal outputs. The results were comparable to those obtained from the blank samples. Furthermore, when these interfering substances coexisted with ICP, both signals remained almost unchanged, indicating that the sensor possessed excellent anti-interference properties. Besides, the specificity of the sensor was largely attributed to the integration with the immunosensor, and the results were presented in Fig. 4C and D, where only the ICP was capable of producing a more significant signal change.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ICP in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The sensor was further applied to recovery experiments using three samples—apple, cowpea, and tap water—to assess its reliability and applicability for detecting ICP in real samples. Complex matrices in food samples and organic reagent residues from sample extraction can interfere with experimental results and affect antigen–antibody recognition to varying degrees. To minimize matrix effects, the experiment involved diluting the matrix solution in PBS buffer at different factors to simulate a buffer environment. The dilution that obtained a curve and IC50 value closest to the buffer calibration curve was selected for further experiments as it was deemed to eliminate matrix interference. Specifically, matrix effects were negligible when the apple and tap water matrices were diluted 5-fold in PBS buffer, while the cowpea matrix required a 20-fold dilution (Fig. 4E–G). Consequently, the detection range of this sensor for ICP residues in samples was determined to be 4.8–156.2 ng g−1. The Chinese national standard (GB 2763-2021) MRLs for ICP are 0.05 mg kg−1 in bean vegetables and 0.01 mg kg−1 in fruits. The practical detection sensitivity of this sensor was still sufficient to meet the MRLs of ICP after considering the sample matrix. As listed in Table S3, the recovery range of spiked ICP was 97.9–105.5 % for the fluorescence signal and 94.8–119.8 % for the photothermal signal, both with RSDs below 13 %. Furthermore, the results from both methods were in good agreement with the GC–MS analysis. This demonstrated that the dual-mode immunosensor was capable of accurately detecting ICP in real samples.

We successfully developed a ratiometric fluorescence and photothermal dual-mode immunosensor based on MnO2NSs to realize the specific detection of ICP. The immunosensor demonstrated high accuracy and strong anti-interference capabilities across both detection modes. It allowed an initial semi-quantitative judgment based on temperature changes and was then combined with the signal of ratiometric fluorescence to analyze the specific concentration of ICP. Satisfactory recoveries were obtained in the real detection of apple, cowpea, and tap water to demonstrate its practical applicability. By utilizing the various sensing properties of a single substance, MnO2NSs, it is feasible to construct a versatile sensing platform. This integration of different signal outputs minimized false-positive results and enhanced the accuracy of detection. In conclusion, this work presents a novel strategy for the sensitive detection of ICP, providing new insights into the potential of dual-mode immunosensors for further applications. However, the two signals in this work cannot be acquired simultaneously. Developing sensing apparatuses capable of detecting multiple signals is an effective strategy to address the challenges of in situ detection and enable faster data processing.

Jie Liu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Hao Deng: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Bao-Zhu Jia: Formal analysis, Data curation. Ze-Shan Lin: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Yu Wang: Resources, Methodology. Hong Wang: Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Zhen-Lin Xu: Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization. Lin Luo: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Research Fund Program of Key Laboratory of Tropical Fruit and Vegetable Cold-chain of Hainan Province/Institute of Agro-products Processing and Design (HAAS2023PT0210), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2023A1515030011).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.157951.

ce:section.ce:para:
